Chapter 4 Random Variables 4.1 Discrete Distributions We now come to an extension of the simple probability model which is very useful. This is the concept of a random variable. We have implicitly already used this concept with our virtual dice and in our example and project on investing and updating believes. Let \({\cal S}\) be some finite set - a sample space - and \(P(x)\) a probability on \({\cal S}\). A random variable is a function assigning numbers to points of \({\cal S}\). The notation \({\cal S}\) has been chosen deliberately to connect this lecture with the previous ones. So we get the following definition Random variable A random variable is a function defined on the sample space. The term random variable might be slightly confusing, for what is really meant is something like a random function where the range is the sample space, i.e. the outcome of a random experiment, and the image is the set of real numbers. Think of the payoff promise in the investment game, we analyzed in lecture 3. The sample space for the flip of a coin is the set \({\cal S}=\{H, T\}\). They payoff for a player who has won the auction for a flip is: 2, if the coin lands on heads, 0 if the coin lands on tails. This uncertain payoff promise can be formalized as a function from \({\cal S}\) to \(\{2,0\}\), with the rule that H is mapped to 2 and T is mapped to 0. It is a widely held convention in probability theory to denote random variables by capital letters, like \(X\) and \(Y\) and their values are denoted by \(x\) and \(y\) respectively. Let \(X\) be a random variable and let \(x_1, x_2, \dots , x_n\) denote the values it can assume. The aggregate of all sample points on which \(X\) assumes the fixed value \(X = x_j\) forms the event that \(X = x_j\). Its probability is denoted by \(P(X = x_j)\). When a random variable can only take finitely many or uncountably many values, i.e. when the sample space contains only finitely many or uncountably many basic outcomes, we say that the random variable is discrete. Probability distribution The function \(P(X = x_j) = p(x_j)\) for \(j = 1, \cdots ,n\) is called the (probability) distribution of the random variable \(X\). This function completely describes the probability properties of the random variable. Note that \(p(x_j) \geq 0\) and \(\sum_{j=1}^n p(x_j) = 1\). A related concept is found in the follwing important definition Cumulative distribution The cumulative distribution function - abbreviated CDF - shows the probability that a random variable \(X\) take a value less than or equal to a given value \(x\). It is usually denoted as \(F(x) = P(x \leq x)\) where \(-\infty < x < \infty\). We have actually already encountered a random variable in our very first lecture, where we discussed the six sided fair die. The random variable \(X\) is in this case a map (the identity map) from the six basic outcomes \({\cal S}=\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\) to the number of points \(\{1,2,3,4,5\}\). If the die is fair, each of these outcomes has probability \(1/6\). It is common to display the probabilities associated with a random variables, graphically as a probability distribution. Figure 4.1: The probability distribution of the random variable of rolling a six sided die The cumulative distribution function of this random variable can be visualized as a step function: Figure 4.2: The cumulative distribution function of the random variable of rolling a six sided die The notion of a distribution can be generalized to more than one random variable. Consider two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\), defined on the same sample space \({\cal S}\). Denote the values they assume by \(x_1, x_2, x_3, ..., x_n\) and \(y_1, y_2, y_3, ..., y_m\) respectively. Let the corresponding distributions be \(f(x_i)\) and \(g(y_j)\). The aggregate points in which the two conditions \(X = x_i\) and \(Y = y_j\) are satisfied form an event whose probability is denoted by \(P(X = x_i, Y = y_j\)) Joint probability function The function \[\begin{eqnarray*} P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) = p(x_i, y_j), \quad i = 1, \cdots, n \,\, j = 1, \cdots, m \end{eqnarray*}\] is called the joint probability distribution of \(X\) and \(Y\). Note that we must have \[\begin{eqnarray*} p(x_i, y_j) &\geq& 0, \quad i = 1, \cdots, n \,\, j = 1, \cdots, m \\ \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j = 1}^m p(x_i, x_j) &=& 1 \end{eqnarray*}\] Moreover for every fixed \(i\) and for every fixed \(j\) we have: \[\begin{eqnarray*} p(x_i, y_1) + p(x_i, y_2) + \cdots + p(x_i, y_m)&=&P(X = x_i)&=&f(x_i)\\ p(x_1, y_j) + p(x_2, y_j) + \cdots + p(x_n, y_j)&=&P(Y = y_j)&=&g(x_j) \end{eqnarray*}\] If you go back to lecture 3, where we discussed conditional probability, this should look very familiar. These notions carry over to the concepts of random variables. With the notion of a joint probability we also get a notion of conditional probability for random variables: Conditional probability The conditional probability of the event \(Y = y_j\) given that \(X = x_i\) is \[\begin{equation*} P(Y = y_j | X = x_i) = \frac{p(x_i, y_l)}{f(x_i)} \end{equation*}\] where we need to require that \(f(x_i) > 0\). With this definition, a number is associated with every value of \(X\) and so conditional probability defines a function of \(X\). This function is called the conditional distribution of \(Y\) for given \(X\). Mutually independent Two random variables are called mutually independent if for any combination of values \(x_1, ..., x_n\), \(y_1, ..., y_m\) \[\begin{equation*} P(X = x_i, Y = y_j) = P(X = x_i) P(Y = y_j) \end{equation*}\] 4.2 Expected value Sometimes the entire probability distribution is difficult to grasp at once. It is common to describe probability distributions therefore by a summary measure. Among the typical summary measures the expectation or the mean is by far the most important one. Expected Value The expected value \(\mathrm{E}(X)\) of a discrete random variable \(X\) is the probability weighted sum of all its possible values: \[\begin{equation*} \mathrm{E}(X) = x_1p(x_1) + x_2 p(x_2) + \cdots + x_n p(x_n) \end{equation*}\] Note that in the definition we have covered the case where the number of possible outcomes is finite. In the other case where the number of possible outcomes is countably infinite we need additional convergence conditions for the infinite series defined by the sum of the \(x_i p(x_i)\). We ignore this case here. The term expected value can be a bit misleading, for it might suggest that the expected value is something we “expect” to occur. It is - to the contrary - pretty often unlikely and sometimes even impossible. As an example, remember our fair die. Its expected value is, if you apply the definition above: 1*1/6+2*1/6+3*1/6+4*1/6+5*1/6+6*1/6 ## [1] 3.5 a value that will never actually come up. We will discuss the specific properties of expected value within an extended example a bit later in this lecture. 4.3 Variation While the expected value of a random variable provides a very useful summary measure for a distribution, we typically also want information about the amount of variation of a a random variable. One such measure, which gives us a degree of possible deviation from the mean is the variance. Variance The variance of a random variable \(X\) is the expected value of the squared deviation from the expected value of \(X\) or \(Var[X] = \mathrm{E}[(X -\mathrm{E}[X])^2]\) Note that we cab simplify the expression for the variance as follows: \[\begin{align*} \mathrm{E}((X-\mathrm{E}(X)^2)) & = \mathrm{E}(X^2 - 2 X \mathrm{E}(X) + \mathrm{E}(X)^2) \\ & = \mathrm{E}(X^2) - 2 \mathrm{E}(X)\mathrm{E}(X) + \mathrm{E}(X)^2 \\ & = \mathrm{E}(X^2) - \mathrm{E}(X)^2 \end{align*}\] In the case of a discrete random variable, the case we discuss in this lecture, we have \(\mathrm{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(x_i - \mathrm{E}(X) \right)^2 p(x_i))\). Note that the units of the variance are the squared units of the random variable. This is often not very useful for interpretations. This leads us to the concept of a standard deviation: Standard Deviation The standard deviation of a random variable \(X\) is the square root of the variance of \(X\), denoted \(\mathrm{SD}(X) = \sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(X)}\). Let’s discuss a few properties of the variance also in the context of the example. In this way we keep faithful to the approach of this lecture to make the abstract concepts tangible. Assume we have two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\) then we define their covariance as follows. Covariance The ++covariance** for two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\), is defined as \[\begin{equation*} \mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathrm{E}((X - \mathrm{E}(X))(Y-\mathrm{E}(Y))) \end{equation*}\] Note that \(\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = \mathrm{E}(XY) - \mathrm{E}(X)\mathrm{E}(Y)\). If \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent, then \(\mathrm{E}(XY) = \mathrm{E}(X)\mathrm{E}(Y)\), hence if \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent, \(\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y) = 0\). Note, however, that the converse is not true. A covariance of 0 does not imply independence. The choice of an origin and unit of measurement of a random variable is to a large degree arbitrary. It is thus often most convenient to take the mean as the origin and the standard deviation as unit. Denote the mean of a random variable \(X\) as \(\mu\) and the variance as \(\sigma^2\). In this case \(X-\mu\) has mean zero and variance \(\sigma^2\), and hence the variable \(X^* = \frac{(X-\mu)}{\sigma}\) has mean 0 and variance 1. If we take the covariance of two such normalized random variables we get the so called correlation coefficient \(\rho(X,Y)\). Correlation Coefficient The correlation coefficient of two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\) is defined as \[\begin{equation*} \rho(X,Y) = \mathrm{Cov}(X^*,Y^*) = \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y} \end{equation*}\] The correlation coefficient is thus just a particular way of writing the covariance. Now it is time we get back to R and play around with these concepts a bit. As a finance class you should be motivated to know these concepts well because they are a sort of cap-stone for investment science and portfolio management. Indeed random variables are the most important models in Finance. In the next example we will study the concepts of this section by putting them in a Finance context. In doing so we will also embark into a new territory of R. We will now also learn the basics of writing R programs using portfolio and investment theory as an example. 4.4 Random variables: The cornerstone for modeling investments under uncertainty An important case where probability is applied in Finance is the problem of analyzing risky investment decisions: When you make an investment with a known initial outlay of capital the amount to be returned from the investment in the future is unknown. In some cases, say when you invest in very liquid assets classes which are constantly traded on markets, like stocks and bonds, you might have an idea on the probability of certain return prospects for investment informed by past data. This case of random returns is one of the most intensively used applications of probability to Finance. Since random variables are the models of choice in this case, we study such an investment problem now. It is at the same time an ideal examles to push the frontier of your knowledge of R, because we can discuss in this context how to write programs and run more complex simulations than we did so far. Let us focus first on a class of investment problems where we only have a single investment period: Money is invested at an initial time and a payoff is attained at the end of this period. Random variables are a concept that allows us to construct models for thinking about and analyzing the uncertainty inherent in such practical situation. While the assumption of a single period is an idealization that does not apply to public stocks such models are applied to this common class of investments as a simplification. For other situations the one period model is sometimes a pretty good approximation. Think of a zero-coupon bond that will be held to maturity. Or think about an investment in a physical project that only will yield a payoff when completed. These kinds of simplifications are - by the way - a cornerstone in modelling as well as in programming. We build solutions to complex problems by starting with a simple problem and then buid complexity step by step. 4.4.1 Asset return Before we dive into the modelling of investment problems with uncertainty using probability and random variables as a tool, we need to introduce some financial concepts first: An investment instrument that can be bought and sold is called an asset in Finance. If \(X_0\) is the amount invested today and \(X_1\) is the amount received “tomorrow,” say for example in a year from now, the total return is defined as the ratio of the amount received to the amount invested or formally \(R = \frac{X_1}{X_0}\). Often we simply use the term return. The rate of return is defined as the difference between the amount received and the amount invested in relation to the amount invested or \(r = \frac{X_1-X_0}{X_0}\). Often people also speak of return when they refer to the rate of return. Both notions are related by the equation \(R = 1+r\) and the equation for the rate of return can be rewritten as \(X_1 = (1+r)X_0\). This shows that the rate of return acts much like an interest rate. When an asset is initially acquired, its rate of return is uncertain. Modelling the rate of return as a random variable is one possibility to describe and analyze this situation of uncertain returns. 4.4.2 Random asset returns Now you can already see, how random variables can act as a model for investment under uncertainty. In an uncertain situation, the amount received at the end of the period \(X_1\) is a random variable. The underlying sample space has to be specified appropriately and there are various ways to do so in an applied situation. We will soon go in detail through an example with a discrete sample space. For returns, it is - of course - more natural to have a continuous sample space, as we will see in the next lecture. If \(X_1\) is a random variable, so is of course \(R\) and \(r\) as a consequence and we are in an investment situation with radom returns. 4.5 Modelling random asset returns: First steps in R programing We are now going to build a model of random returns that we can use to simulate the payoff consequences of portfolio investment decisions as well as for giving a concrete context to the abstract notions we introduced before. This will show you their power as well as their practical relevance in Finance. How do actual returns look like? Let’s go back to the example of the S&P 500 - a broad stock market index - we discussed before. sp500 <- tq_get("^GSPC", get = "stock.prices", from = "2011-01-03", to = "2021-12-22") head(sp500, n = 5) ## # A tibble: 5 x 8 ## symbol date open high low close volume adjusted ## <chr> <date> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ## 1 ^GSPC 2011-01-03 1258. 1276. 1258. 1272. 4286670000 1272. ## 2 ^GSPC 2011-01-04 1273. 1274. 1263. 1270. 4796420000 1270. ## 3 ^GSPC 2011-01-05 1269. 1278. 1265. 1277. 4764920000 1277. ## 4 ^GSPC 2011-01-06 1276. 1278. 1270. 1274. 4844100000 1274. ## 5 ^GSPC 2011-01-07 1274. 1277. 1262. 1272. 4963110000 1272. Now let’s use R to add a column with daily returns based on the variable adjusted the adjusted index value, which takes into account dividend payments and other things. Our knowldege of how to manipulate R objects by subsetting rules and the application of functions is already there from our lecture on conditional probability: We add a column of first differences \(X_{t+1} - X_t\) for \(t \in \{2, \cdots ,2762\}\). We have to start at the second row because for the first observation we have no previous observation to deduct. Thus when we add a new column to the data frame we need a column of equal length, so we put an NA in the beginning of the returns column. We then have to divide by \(X_t\) for \(t \in \{ 1,\cdots, 2761\}\) because for the last observation we have no next observation to deduct the last one from and divide by it. Remember that we can refer to elements in an R object by indexing with numbers. If we apply the length()function to an object we get the number of components and thus the number of the last element in an object. This we drop. The first difference we already know how to deal with. So let’s add returns sp500$returns <- c(NA, (diff(sp500$adjusted, lag = 1)/sp500$adjusted[-length(sp500$adjusted)])) Now it is always a good idea when you write complex commands like this to check whether the code is doing what you want. The second and the first value of the adjustedvariable is: 1270.199951 and 1271.869995. Thus the return is -0.0013131. The first value in our returns variable is: sp500$returns[2] ## [1] -0.001313062 This looks good. Now let us use our little graphical knowledge to look at the distribution of returns in our data using the hist()function from base R and using the option freq = TRUE to get empirical frequencies. We drop the NA and : hist(sp500$returns[-1]) This picture suggests that (daily) returns are pretty much distributed symmetrically around 0, and in very rare cases rise and drop a bit more than 5 % per day. Note that this is a histogram, which means that we binned the return data into buckets of 1 % and plotted it. It would be natural to assume a continuous sample space for modeling returns. We will discuss such models in the next lecture. Here we take a choice where we model randomness using discrete random variables. We do so by combining two random devices, constructed as a wheel of fortune. Both kinds of wheels are useful for the study of investments. Our wheel of fortune looks like this: Figure 4.3: A wheel of fortune. If the wheel stops at the segment under the marker your payoff is determined according to the rules of the game If you invest an amount of money in the wheel, your payoff will be determined according to the rules of the game depending which segment ends up under the marker (the triangle above the wheel). The wheel is constructed such that it stops at segment \(H\) with probability \(\frac{1}{3}\), at \(L\) with probability \(\frac{1}{6}\) and at \(N\) with probability \(\frac{1}{2}\). There is another random device, before the wheel is spun, which is a coin, which is flipped and ends up on \(B\) with probability \(\frac{3}{5}\) or \(R\) with probability \(\frac{2}{5}\). This creates in total 6 outcomes \({\cal S} = \{BH, BL, BN, RH, RL, RN\}\) with probabilities \(p(x) = \{ 0.2, 0.1, 0.3, 0.13, 0.07, 0.2\}\). In this game you have some initial wealth, which you may invest into 3 alternative assets. They have a payoff-structure described in the following tables: If the coin lands on \(B\): Wheel Asset 1 Asset 2 Asset 3 H 1 10 6 L 1 2 4 N 1 6 5 If the coin lands on \(R\): Wheel Asset 1 Asset 2 Asset 3 H 1 5 3 L 0 1 2 N 1 3 2.5 You can allocate your wealth between the three assets and have to pay the following prices per unit \(q = \{0.93, 5.86, 4.625 \}\). You can not invest more than your initial wealth of 1000. You can neither sell nor short sell any of the assets. You may think of these assets as a bond an two stocks. Asset 1, the bond, pays a fixed amount in each state, except when things go very bad and it defaults and pays nothing. Asset 2 and Asset 3 could be thought of as two stocks where Asset 2 has a higher variation in its payments across states. These are the rules of the game. Now here is the programming challenge we undertake in several steps: We build a function that randomly chooses an outcome according to the probabilities we have specified and then determine the payoff of each asset according to the rules of the game. When we are finished with this step it will look something like this: When you give your portfolio to the outcome() function you will get the state, the payoff of the different assets and the return. outcome(portfolio) The first step in this program, you already know from previous lectures. Let us call this function get_state(). We have to randomly draw a state by flipping the coin and spinning the wheel, just as we rolled the dice in lecture 1: get_state <- function(){ coin <- c("B", "R") wheel <- c("H", "L", "N") state <- c(sample(coin, size = 1, replace = T, prob = c(3/5, 2/5)), sample(wheel, size = 1, replace = T, prob = c(1/3, 1/6, 1/2))) |> paste(collapse = "") return(state) } In this program, we have created inside the function a coin and a wheel, determined a state vector by sampling form the coin and the wheel according to our probabilities and then concatenate the two draws - the two strings - by using the paste() function. Here we have used R’s pipe operator |>, which channels the output of c() into the paste()function as an argument. Use the help menu to check how it works in detail. We can now use get_stateto flip the coin and spin the wheel to generate a state of the world: get_state() ## [1] "BN" The monetary value of the outcome depends on the portfolio of assets you have chosen. We write a program to compute the payoff and save it as a function, so for instance, if we get payoff("RH", portfolio) will output the payoffs and returns or the portfolio you have chosen. After that the full portfolio simulator will look something like this outcome <- function(portfolio){ state <- get_state() a vector showing the state and the portfolio return computed by a function payoff(state, portfolio) } The print() function prints output to the console window, and allows us to display messages from within the body of a function. Note that in lecture 1 we showed and encouraged you to write all of your R code in an R script, a text file where you can compose your code. This will become very important as you work through this part of lecture 4. Remember that you can open an R script in RStudio by going to the menu bar and clicking on File > New File > R Script. 4.5.1 A strategy for writing a program A coding task like writing a function to determine the random outcome of an investment decision is a complex task. Such a task can be tackled best, by using a fairly simple strategy: Break complex tasks into simple sub-tasks Use concrete examples for testing Describe your solution first in words or a picture before you convert it into code. A program is a step by step instruction to your computer to perform certain tasks. All instructions together will accomplish something sophisticated, but taken apart, each single step will likely to be straightforward. Writing a program is easier when you break it into sub-tasks and work on them separately first. R programs contain typically two types of subtasks, sequential steps and parallel cases. The sequential steps we have already identified. We give the portfolio to the function, it draws a state, displays the result and then computes the portfolio payoff and the return. Parallel cases occur when there are groups of similar processes within a task. Some tasks also require different algorithms for different kinds of input. If you can find these groups, you can work out the algorithms one at a time. For example, payoff() will need to compute the portfolio payoff and the return, depending on the state. In all cases in our example, though, the function will use the same algorithm. 4.5.2 if Statements and if-else Statements When you have to link parallel cases together, the program faces a fork in its process whenever it has to choose between cases. If statements help the program to do so. An if statememt in R tells R to do certain things for a particular case. The sytnax of telling R to do something if a case occurs is: if (this) { that } So an example in our case would be: if (state == "BH"){ print("BH") } The condition in an if statement must evaluate to a single TRUEof FALSE. If statements don’t have to be limited to a single line of code. Between the braces there can be arbitrary lines. if-statements tell R what to do if a condition is true. But you can also tell R what to do if a condition is false. A simple code example would be: if (this){ Plan A } else { Plan B } If the case has more than two mutually exclusive cases, you can string multiple if and else statements together by adding an if statement immediately after the else. R will work then through if conditions until one evaluates to TRUE and then ignore the rest. Now this suggests to handle the cases of different states for the portfolio like this: if (state == "BN"){ return <- sum(c(1/q1 -1 ,10/q2 -1 ,6/q3 -1 )*portfolio) } else if (state == "BH"){ return <- sum(c(1/q1 -1 ,6/q2 -1 ,5/q3 -1 )*portfolio) } else if (state == "BL"){ return <- sum(c(1/q1 -1 ,2/q2 -1 ,4/q3 -1 )*portfolio) } else if (state == "RN"){ return <- sum(c(1/q1 -1 ,3/q2 -1 ,2.5/q3 -1 )*portfolio) } else if (state == "RH"){ return <- sum(c(0/q1 -1 ,1/q2 -1 , 2/q3 -1 )*portfolio) } else if (state == "RL"){ return <- sum(c(0/q1 -1 ,1/q2 -1 , 2/q3 -1 )*portfolio) } While this method works, it is for various reasons not very elegant. The first unsatisfactory thing here is that the payoff numbers are hard coded into the if statement. if we would like to consider other examples, or different payoff structures, we would have to rewrite the entire if statement. This is tedious and prone to mistakes. The second not so elegant thing about this code chunk is that we need to compose a multiplication and an addition for computing the payoff. It would be more efficient, if we could do this in a single operation. Finally the cascade of if else statements is quite unwieldy and difficult to read. Maybe we can do better than that? One way to address concern 1 would be to define portfolio payoffs and portfolios outside of the function as abstract objects, so our function will work with any payoff structure. The second concern can be addressed if we reveal the linear algebra nature of the problem and unleash R’s power to deal with linear algebra problems. The address of the third concern, will lead us towards the concept of lookup-tables. The asset payoffs could be mathematically represented as a matrix, where the rows are the states and the columns are the assets. We might also add an extra column with prices to our matrix, so we have all the data in one object How do we specify the portfolio? We have an initial wealth and the wealth is invested into the three assets. We do not say anything here how the allocation is decided but once a decision has been taken, the portfolio will be a vector of weights \((w^1, w^2, w^3)\) with \(0 \leq w^i \leq 1\) for \(i = 1,2,3\). 4.5.3 Portfolios and linear algebra Let us address the first issue and find an abstract representation of our portfolio. A very efficient and convenient way for this is to think of the payoffs in terms of linear algebra as a matrix. For example, the matrix of asset payoffs when the coin lands on \(B\) could be written as a \(3 \times 3\) matrix where the rows show the payoff of each asset, in each of the states \(N,H,L\) chosen by the wheel of fortune. \[\begin{equation*} A_B = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 10 & 6 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \end{equation*}\] Likewise, you can write \[\begin{equation*} A_R = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 5 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 & 2.5 \end{pmatrix} \end{equation*}\] Since we have two random devices combined, the coin and the wheel we ultimately get six possible outcomes. Our state space is \({\cal S} = \{BH, BN, BL, RH, RN, RL\}\). Thus if we combine all possible payoffs in a matrix we get a \(6 \times 3\) matrix, where we have a row for each outcome in the state space and a column for each asset. If you still remember what we discussed in lecture 2, you know that we can store objects in R as a matrix. We do this by telling R that we now specify a matrix and tell the column and row numbers to R and show the \(A_B\) matrix to see what we get A <- matrix(c(1,10,6,1,2,4,1,6,5,1,5,3,0,1,2,1,3,2.5), nrow = 6, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE) A ## [,1] [,2] [,3] ## [1,] 1 10 6.0 ## [2,] 1 2 4.0 ## [3,] 1 6 5.0 ## [4,] 1 5 3.0 ## [5,] 0 1 2.0 ## [6,] 1 3 2.5 Note two things here. We gave all the matrix entries as one vector of values, where we read the entries from left to right going form the first to the last column, starting with the payoffs in B and then going on to R. Then we specify the dimensions of the matrix. Finally we set the byrow argument to TRUE to tell R that it should fill up the values by row instead of its default behavior which would be a fill up by column. Now the R system shows us abstract row and column indices. It would be easier for us to read the matrices correctly, if we had names as references. Here the attribute system of R helps us, for an object of class matrixcan have row and column names as attributes which we can set using appropriate functions. This names our rows and columns but otherwise leaves the mathematical properties of \(A\) untouched. We can do matrix computations with it like we had no names. Let’s do this and see what we get by inspecting \(A\) again colnames(A) <- c("asset_1", "asset_2", "asset_3") rownames(A) <- c("BH", "BL", "BN", "RH", "RL", "RN") A ## asset_1 asset_2 asset_3 ## BH 1 10 6.0 ## BL 1 2 4.0 ## BN 1 6 5.0 ## RH 1 5 3.0 ## RL 0 1 2.0 ## RN 1 3 2.5 To compute returns, we also need price information in addition to the information on our asset payoffs. Our prices are \(q = \{0.93, 5.86, 4.625 \}\). Now with this approach we can now address point 1. and 2: With this construction we could write our payoff function maybe like this. It turns our asset-payoffs into returns: asset_returns <- function(assets, prices){ # construct auxiliary matrix by stacking the price vector to get a matrix with similar # dimensions as assets aux <- matrix(rep(prices, dim(assets)[1]), nrow = dim(assets)[1], ncol = dim(assets)[2], byrow = T) # Compute return rates by component wise division of assets with aux and deduct 1 from each # component r <- (assets/aux) - 1 r } Let us go through this function step by step: The arguments are the payoff matrix and the asset prices. Then we create an object inside the function, we call aux (an abbreviation for auxialliary). To compute returns we have to divide every payoff by the price we paid. We do this by constructing an auxiliary matrix with the same dimensions as the payoff matrix ( 6 times 3). We then make a matrix, telling R to fill the matrix row-wise. The number of repetitions of the price vector we need is equal to the number of rows of our asset matrix. This number can be retrieved by the command dim(assets)[1] because dim applied to the asset matrix will return two numbers, the number of rows and the number of columns.It allows us to apply a component wise division and use R’s recycling rules to turn the matrix of asset payoffs and the vector of prices into a matrix of returns. Your see here that we can assign the particular values we need for this computation outside of this function. This allows us to compute returns for any value of the arguments. No specific values are hardcoded into the function. Notice also the lines, which begin with a # symbol. These are comments. Writing comments into your code should become second nature for you, when you write code. It describes in words what you write in R. It helps you to communicate with others, who might read your code. This includes your future self. As you gain more routine in writing little programs and procedures you will experience that upon returning to your code from a dew weeks ago you sometimes do not understand anymore what you were doing. Comments help you to avoid such confusion. In R the escape character for comments is #. Every line beginning with this symbol will be understood to contain comments by R and is not going to be processed by the function. Let us test this function on our data: assets <- A prices <- c(0.93, 5.86, 4.625) asset_returns(assets, prices) ## asset_1 asset_2 asset_3 ## BH 0.07526882 0.70648464 0.29729730 ## BL 0.07526882 -0.65870307 -0.13513514 ## BN 0.07526882 0.02389078 0.08108108 ## RH 0.07526882 -0.14675768 -0.35135135 ## RL -1.00000000 -0.82935154 -0.56756757 ## RN 0.07526882 -0.48805461 -0.45945946 4.5.4 Lookup Tables Often the simplest way to do something in R involves subsetting. Our function gives us a column vector with rownames for the returns in each basic outcome of our random experiment. returns <- asset_returns(assets, prices) returns ## asset_1 asset_2 asset_3 ## BH 0.07526882 0.70648464 0.29729730 ## BL 0.07526882 -0.65870307 -0.13513514 ## BN 0.07526882 0.02389078 0.08108108 ## RH 0.07526882 -0.14675768 -0.35135135 ## RL -1.00000000 -0.82935154 -0.56756757 ## RN 0.07526882 -0.48805461 -0.45945946 If we know, for instance, that the state is \(BH\) we can extract the state by returns["BH", ] ## asset_1 asset_2 asset_3 ## 0.07526882 0.70648464 0.29729730 This opens an option to avoid the complicated if tree. Assume a state is chosen by our get_state() function. state <- get_state() Then we can compute the portfolio return for this state by subsetting: returns[state, ] ## asset_1 asset_2 asset_3 ## 0.07526882 0.70648464 0.29729730 The vector of row names rownames(A) ## [1] "BH" "BL" "BN" "RH" "RL" "RN" functions as a so called lookup table. It is a complete list of the possible states. R can refer to this state by subsetting and choose the right row. In the end we want to keep just the return number. We can strip of the name by the unname() function. This helps us to avoid a complicated if-else-tree in this case. 4.5.5 Writing the outcome program Now we can put together the different parts to implement our envisaged skeleton outcome <- function(portfolio){ state <- get_state() a vector showing the state and the asset returns computed by a function payoff(state, portfolio) } We have broken this down to sub-tasks which we now weave together: ret <- asset_returns(assets, prices) outcome <- function(returns = ret){ # Draw a state by flipping the coin and spinning the wheel state <- get_state() # Select the returns for this state and strip off names return <- unname(ret[state, ]) # pack result into a data frame data.frame(State = state, Asset_1 = return[1], Asset_2 = return[2], Asset_3 = return[3], stringsAsFactors = FALSE) } Let us test this function using the values we have already specified: a <- A pr <- c(0.93, 5.86, 4.625) ret <- asset_returns(assets = a, prices = pr) outcome(returns = ret) ## State Asset_1 Asset_2 Asset_3 ## 1 BN 0.07526882 0.02389078 0.08108108 This does not look so bad, but what can we do with this? Well now we are in the position to study a particular portfolio through simulation. We can play with the coin and the wheel random mechanism to see what it does to our asset returns and to our portfolio returns. We can study the expected return and the variation of returns. For this we need another flow control construct 4.5.6 for Loops A for loop is a command that repeats a chunk of code many times, once for each element in a set of inputs. A for loop tells R “do this for every value of that.” In the syntax of R this looks like this: for (value in that) { this } The thatobject should be a set of objects (often a vector of numbers or character strings). The for loop will run the code that appears between the braces once for each member of that. For example, the for loop below runs print("one run") once for each element in the vector of character strings: for (value in c("My", "first", "for", "loop")){ print("one run") } ## [1] "one run" ## [1] "one run" ## [1] "one run" ## [1] "one run" The valuesymbol in a for loop acts like an argument in a function. The for loop will create an object named valueand assign it a new value on each run of the loop. The code in your loop can access this value by calling the valueobject. What values will the loop assign to value? It will use the elements in the set that you run the loop on. for starts with the first element and then assigns a different element to valueon each run of the for loop, until all of the elements have been assigned to value. For example, the for loop below will run print(value) four times and will print out one element of c("My", "second", "for", "loop")each time: for (value in c("My", "second", "for", "loop")){ print(value) } ## [1] "My" ## [1] "second" ## [1] "for" ## [1] "loop" Of course there is nothing special in using the name value. Any other name will do as well. If you look at the valueafter the loop runs, you will see that it still contains the value of the last element in the set. Usually a for loop won’t return anything, unless you save its own output as it runs. The examples before appeared to return something becuase we called the print() function at every call. if we typed for example: for(i in c("My", "third", "for", "loop")){ value } we get no output. To get an output you must fill either a vector or a list, which will then contain the output of every iteration in the for loop. Let’s see how this works in the next example: chars <- vector(mode = "character", length = 4) words <- c("My", "forth", "for", "loop") for( i in 1:4){ chars[i] <- words[i] } chars ## [1] "My" "forth" "for" "loop" What is going on here? We have first preallocated a vector of type character with length 4. The vector does not yet contain anything. Then we declared the four words we are going to use in our loop and saved them in an object called “word.” Then we start the loop and save at each of the four slots in the character vector one word from “words,” one after the other. At the end of the loop chars is filled with the words from word and can be inspected. 4.5.7 Portfolio expected Return and Variance Let us now analyze the expected return and the variance of our portfolio using a simulation approach by writing a for loop and by using our outcome function. Our horizon for computing returns is one day. So let us look at the expected daily return over a period similar to our SP500 data. This comprised 2762 trading days. So let’s write a for loop with length 2762 and show the states and the asset returns on each day. # Preallocate a list in which we then can store the values of the for loop simulated_returns <- vector(mode = "list", length = 2762) # Transform payoffs into returns returns <- asset_returns(assets = A, prices = prices) # Compute 250 random outcomes and random returns for(i in 1:2762){ simulated_returns[[i]] <- outcome(returns) } Now let’s inspect the output of this run by looking at the first element: simulated_returns[[1]] ## State Asset_1 Asset_2 Asset_3 ## 1 BL 0.07526882 -0.6587031 -0.1351351 This looks good. We now have a list with 250 data-frames all with the same variables. What we would like to have instead is one single data frame. Here is a way how to merge them: simulation_outcome <- do.call(rbind, simulated_returns) head(simulation_outcome, n = 5) ## State Asset_1 Asset_2 Asset_3 ## 1 BL 0.07526882 -0.6587031 -0.1351351 ## 2 BH 0.07526882 0.7064846 0.2972973 ## 3 BL 0.07526882 -0.6587031 -0.1351351 ## 4 RH 0.07526882 -0.1467577 -0.3513514 ## 5 RL -1.00000000 -0.8293515 -0.5675676 Let me explain. Dataframes with the same variables can be merged by the function rbind(). This can be applied to an arbitrary (finite) number of dataframes. do.call() is a function which is applied to a list. Its first argument is a function (what should be done), the second argument is a list (to what should the function be applied). The call of this functions binds all the data frames stored in our list together in one single frame. Now let us examine our simulated output a bit further. First let us check whether the relative frequencies of states are near the values of the probabilities with which they should occur given our coin and wheel random device: rel_freq_states <- prop.table(table(simulation_outcome$State)) rel_freq_states ## ## BH BL BN RH RL RN ## 0.20709631 0.09847936 0.30774801 0.13323678 0.06010138 0.19333816 Remember from before that our theoretical probability distribution was given by \[\begin{eqnarray*} {\cal S} &=& \{BH, BL, BN, RH, RL, RN\} \\ p(x) &=& \{0.2, 0.1, 0.3, 0.13, 0.07, 0.2\} \end{eqnarray*}\] This looks pretty good and illustrates what we should expect from the weak law of large numbers. We have given the probabilities by \(p(x)\) and a repetition of 250 independent draws from this distribution results in relative frequencies of the individual states which are already fairly close to these theoretical probabilities. In a “notebook-view,” the expected value can be seen as the long term average of a random variable in a series of a repeated random experiment. Let us check this for our data but using the portfolio weights. We add the weights for each asset and then compute \(E(w^1 r^1), E(w^2 r^2), E(w^3 r^3)\) from our data and compare to the theoretical expected value according to our definition. simulation_outcome$Weight_1 <- 1/4 simulation_outcome$Weight_2 <- 3/8 simulation_outcome$Weight_3 <- 3/8 head(simulation_outcome, n = 5) ## State Asset_1 Asset_2 Asset_3 Weight_1 Weight_2 Weight_3 ## 1 BL 0.07526882 -0.6587031 -0.1351351 0.25 0.375 0.375 ## 2 BH 0.07526882 0.7064846 0.2972973 0.25 0.375 0.375 ## 3 BL 0.07526882 -0.6587031 -0.1351351 0.25 0.375 0.375 ## 4 RH 0.07526882 -0.1467577 -0.3513514 0.25 0.375 0.375 ## 5 RL -1.00000000 -0.8293515 -0.5675676 0.25 0.375 0.375 E_1 <- mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_1*simulation_outcome$Weight_1) E_2 <- mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_2*simulation_outcome$Weight_2) E_3 <- mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_3*simulation_outcome$Weight_3) round(c(E_1, E_2, E_3), 2) ## [1] 0.00 -0.03 -0.04 Let us compare this result with the theoretical expected value. probabilities <- c(0.2, 0.1, 0.3, 0.13, 0.07, 0.2) e_1 <- sum(returns[, "asset_1"]*0.25*probabilities) e_2 <- sum(returns[, "asset_2"]*0.375*probabilities) e_3 <- sum(returns[, "asset_3"]*0.375*probabilities) round(c(e_1, e_2, e_3), 2) ## [1] 0.00 -0.03 -0.04 This looks good. Now Let us take this opportunity to establish a few properties of expected value. These properties are very important and you will need it over and over in applied probability. I hope that with the hands on approach of our simulation setup you will see more easily why they must hold than in a purely formal derivation. 4.5.8 Properties of expected returns and portfolio expected return Expectation is linear: If \(X\) and \(Y\) are random variables then \[\begin{equation*} E(a\,X + b\,Y) = a\,E(X) + b\, E(Y) \end{equation*}\] for any real values of \(a\) and \(b\). Now look at this property from the viewpoint of our data frame. If we computed the empirical expected value by: E_1 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_1)*mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_1) E_2 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_2)*mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_2) E_3 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_3)*mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_3) round(c(E_1, E_2, E_3), 2) ## [1] 0.00 -0.03 -0.04 We get exactly the same value. Note that I have used the function unique()here. This is because if simulation_outcome$Weight_1 is taken out of the mean function it is a \(2762 \times 1\) vector containing the same weight value for every daily observation. Unique reduces this column to a single scalar. The expected return of a portfolio is the weighted return of the expected returns of each asset: This is an immediate consequence of the linearity of expectations, where \(a\) and \(b\) take the roles of weights. This property should jump right into the eye if you think of the problem in terms of our data frame. Clearly, if you take a weighted sum over rows and then average accross this sum it is the same as if you take a weighted average across each column and then sum up. Let’s do it with our data frame: # Version 1: Take expected return of each asset and then compute weighted average: # w^1 E(r^1) + w^2 E(r^2) + w^3 E(r^3) round(E_1 + E_2 + E_3, 2) ## [1] -0.06 # Version 2: Compute E(w^1 r^1 + w^2 r^2 + w^3 r^3) mean(simulation_outcome$Weight_1*simulation_outcome$Asset_1 + simulation_outcome$Weight_2*simulation_outcome$Asset_2 + simulation_outcome$Weight_3*simulation_outcome$Asset_3) |> round(2) ## [1] -0.06 Finally, note that the expected value of a constant is the value of the constant: Property 3: For a constant \(a\), \(E(a) = a\). What about the variance? Let us also use our data frame to establish some important properties. 4.5.9 Properties of variance and portfolio variance Property 1 If \(X\) is a random variable, \[\begin{equation*} Var(X) = E(X^2) - (E(X))^2 \end{equation*}\] Let us check this with the random returns of asset 1: # variance of returns rounded to two digits: var(simulation_outcome$Asset_1) |> round(2) ## [1] 0.07 # variance computed by applying Property 1: (mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_1^2) - mean(simulation_outcome$Asset_1)^2) |> round(2) ## [1] 0.07 Property 2 If \(X\) is a random variable and \(a\) is a real number we have \[\begin{equation*} Var(a\,X) = a^2 Var(X) \end{equation*}\] This says intuitively that if you multiply a random variable by, say 5, then its average squared distance from the mean should increase by a factor of 25. Let us check this again with our data frame for asset 1: # Var(w^1*r^1) var(simulation_outcome$Asset_1*simulation_outcome$Weight_1) |> round(4) ## [1] 0.0041 # (w^1)^2 Var(r^1) ((unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_1))^2 * var(simulation_outcome$Asset_1)) |> round(4) ## [1] 0.0041 Property 3 If \(X\) is a random variable and \(a\) is a constant then \[\begin{equation*} Var(X + a) = Var(X) \end{equation*}\] This should also be intuitive, because a constant has no variation so it should not contribute to the variance of a random variable. Let us check this again with our data frame: # Var(r^1 + 1000) var(simulation_outcome$Asset_1 + 1000) ## [1] 0.06533664 # Var(r^1) var(simulation_outcome$Asset_1) ## [1] 0.06533664 Finally we have a very important property of variance which is particularly relevant in the context of portfolios. Property 4 If \(X\) and \(Y\) are random variables and \(a\) and \(b\) are real numbers \[\begin{equation*} Var(a\,X + b\, Y) = a^2\, Var(X) + b^2 \, Var(Y) + 2 ab \, Cov(X,Y) \end{equation*}\] If \(X\) and \(Y\) are independent, then \(Cov(Y,Y) = 0\) and in this particular case the sum of the variance is equal to the variance of the sum. Now property 4 can be generalized to \(n\) random variables. Let \(r^i\) denote random returns and \(w^i\) portfolio weights. In this case the formula in property 4 becomes: \[\begin{equation*} Var(\sum_i w^i\,r^i) = \sum_{i=1}^n (w^i)^2 Var(r^i) + \sum_{i \neq j } w^i w^j Cov(r^i, r^j) \end{equation*}\] Le us check this formula again with our data frame: # Var(\sum_i w^i\,r^i) aux_1 <- simulation_outcome$Weight_1*simulation_outcome$Asset_1 + simulation_outcome$Weight_2*simulation_outcome$Asset_2 + simulation_outcome$Weight_3*simulation_outcome$Asset_3 var(aux_1) |> round(4) ## [1] 0.0989 # \sum_{i=1}^n (w^i)^2 Var(r^i) + \sum_{i \neq j } w^i w^j Cov(r^i, r^j) w1 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_1) w2 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_2) w3 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_3) Varportf <- w1^2*var(simulation_outcome$Asset_1) + w2^2*var(simulation_outcome$Asset_2) + w3^2*var(simulation_outcome$Asset_3) + w1*w2*cov(simulation_outcome$Asset_1,simulation_outcome$Asset_2) + w1*w3*cov(simulation_outcome$Asset_1, simulation_outcome$Asset_3) + w2*w1*cov(simulation_outcome$Asset_1, simulation_outcome$Asset_2) + w2*w3*cov(simulation_outcome$Asset_2, simulation_outcome$Asset_3) + w3*w1*cov(simulation_outcome$Asset_1, simulation_outcome$Asset_3) + w3*w2*cov(simulation_outcome$Asset_2, simulation_outcome$Asset_3) Varportf |> round(4) ## [1] 0.0989 It works great but this notation is pretty awkward. Here is how we can do better. With more than two random variables it is convenient to use linear algebra or matrix notation and to make use of R’s capabilities to do do. Let me give you a brief a reminder how this works. We let \(x = (x_1, x_2, \cdots, x_n)\) denote a vector of \(n\) random variables. When \(x\) is a column vector, then it’s transpose is a row vector. The usual notation is \(x^T\) for transposition. The covariance associated with a vector \(x\) is most conveniently written as a \(m \times n\) matrix \(C\) where \(C_{ij} = cov(x_im )\). If \(x\) is rewarded as a column vector and \(x^T\) as the corresponding row vector then \(C\) can be expressed as \[\begin{equation*} C = E\left( (x - E(x))(x-E(x))^T \right) \end{equation*}\] If \(w\) is a vector of portfolio weights we can write the variance of the portfolio as \[\begin{equation*} w^T C w \end{equation*}\] If you multiply a matrix by a column vector, you have to take the inner-product of the vector with each row of the matrix, like this: Figure 4.4: When mutilpying a column vector with a matrix take the inner product of the vector with every row of the matrix. The result will be a column vector Likewise if you multiply a row vector with a matrix, you have to (symmetrically) take the inner product of the row vector with each column of the matrix. Lets apply this for the example we wrote down very tediously before. To understand this example, you need to know that in R a vector is just a special matrix, with one column (or one row) and the operator for an inner product is %*%. This is important, because if you use the * operator R will take a component-wise instead of an inner product. Finally, the R-function for transpositions is t(). Armed with this knowledge lets redo the previous example: # w^T%*%(Var(r^1)+Var(r^2)+Var(r^3))%*%w + w^T C w w1 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_1) w2 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_2) w3 <- unique(simulation_outcome$Weight_3) w <- matrix(c(w1,w2,w3), nrow = 3, ncol = 1) R <- cbind(simulation_outcome$Asset_1, simulation_outcome$Asset_2, simulation_outcome$Asset_3) Vp <- t(w)%*% cov(R)%*%w Vp |> round(4) ## [,1] ## [1,] 0.0989 Much more elegant and less error prone, isn’t it? add expectation and variance of functions of a RV \(X\). Our portfolio seems to be a bad deal. Over the course of the holding period it had an average daily return of \(-0.08\) with a standard deviation of 0.33. It has an negative return on average and it is pretty risky. We should be able to do better than that. How we can combine models of random returns with considerations of optimal portfolio choice is subject of a whole body of Finance theory, which you will learn and hear about in other classes. The key words for this body of applied theory is mean variance analysis, CAPM and the Markowitz Model 4.6 The Binomial Distribution and models of asset price dynamics 4.6.1 The binomial distribution in applied computational Finance In the last section of this lecture we discuss one particular discrete distribution: The binomial distribution. This is a discrete distribution which is particularly important in applied work and it is the foundation of one of the most important models of asset price dynamics in applied work in Finance. With respect to R, the discussion of the binomial distribution also provides an important lesson to us. The base version of R has implemented all random variables and their distributions in special functions. We learn how to use these functions. Their syntax is always the same, and thus, once you know one of them you also know the syntax for all the others. 4.6.2 Bernoulli random variables as a building block of the binomial distribution The sample space of a Bernoulli experiment has only two possible outcomes, often denoted as “success” and “failure”: \({\cal S} = \{\text{success}, \text{failure} \}\). We have encountered this random variable already in form of coin flips in lectures 2, 3 and 4. The flipping of a coin would be a typical Bernoulli experiment, where the appearnac of heads could be the success and tails the failure (or the other way round, this is a convention). Bernoulli random variable The bernoulli random variable takes a value of 1 or 0. The probability of success is given \(p\) and of failure \(1-p\). The notation for a bernoulli random variable is \(X \sim Ber(p)\). This means: \(X\) is a random variable that has bernoulli distribution with success probability \(p\) (and thererfore failure probability \(1-p\)). The expected value of a bernoulli random variable \(X\) is \(E(X) = p\) and its variance is \(Var(X) = p (1-p)\). You may try to derive this statement from the definitions of expected value and variance we introduced in this lecture. Binomial random variable The binomial random variable generalizes the idea of a Bernoulli random variable. A binomial random variable \(X\) is the sum of \(n\) independent and identically distributed bernoulli random variables. The random variable is defined by two parameters, the total number of trials \(n\) and the probability of “success” \(p\). It is useful for caclulating the probability of getting \(k\) successes in \(n\) trials. The notation is \(X \sim Bin(n,p)\). The expected value of a binomial random variable \(X\) is \(E(X) = n\,p\) and its variance is \(Var(X) = n\,p\,(1-p)\) To develop a bit of intuition about bernoulli and binomial random variables, let us go back to our virtual die and consider rolling the die five times. The random variable \(X\) we are interested in is the number of sixes occuring in these five rolls. We can figure out the probability that a six is shwon in all five rolls. We can use independence and the multiplication rule: \[\begin{equation*} P(X = 5) = \left( \frac{1}{6} \right)^5 \end{equation*}\] (1/6)^5 ## [1] 0.0001286008 Likewise we can figure out the probability of no sixes \[\begin{equation*} P(X = 0) = \left( \frac{5}{6} \right)^5 \end{equation*}\] (5/6)^5 ## [1] 0.4018776 These cases were easy. But what about the probability of exactly one six? There are five outcomes with \(X = 1\), since the six can occur at the first, the second and so until at the fifth roll. Each of these cases has probability \[\begin{equation*} P(X = 1) = 5 \left( \frac{5}{6} \right)^4 \left( \frac{1}{6} \right) \end{equation*}\] 5*(5/6)^4*(1/6) ## [1] 0.4018776 What about two sixes? You can figure out that there are 10 different ways to get two sixes in five rolls. Generalizing this reasoning leads to a general formula for the probability distribution function of a binomial random variable. If \(X\) is a random variable representing the number of successes, then the probability of getting exactly \(k\) successes is: \[\begin{equation*} P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k \, (1-p)^{(n-k)} \end{equation*}\] \(\binom{n}{k}\) is the binomial coefficient and means \(\frac{n !}{k! (n-k)!}\). You see that the formulas become quickly quite complicated. But here we can reap the advantages of the computer. R has a syntax for all known random variables with a parametric distribution. The syntax is always the same. Let us introduce this syntax for the binomial distribution: dbinom(x,n,p) will return the probability that \(X\) is equal to \(x\) where \(X \sim \textrm{Binom}(n,p)\). This is the distribution function for the binomial random variable. pbinom(x,n,p) will return the probability that \(X\) is less or equal to \(x\), where \(X \sim \textrm{Binom}(n,p)\). This is the cumulative distribution function. rbinom(N,n,p) will return \(N\) randomly generated values from a binomial random variable. qbinom(r,n,p) will return the smallest value \(x\), such that \(P(X \leq x) \geq r\) where \(X \sim \textrm{Binom}(n,p)\). This is called a quantile function and we will learn about its meaning in the next lecture. Let us show an application case for these function from a context we already know. Let us take our dice rolling example. Let us take the example discussed before, the probability of seeing one six in five rolls of a dice. We solved such a problem by simulation in the previous lectures. We write a function replicate it many times and then plot the distribution we get in this way: roll_die_five <- function(){ # create a six sided die die <- 1:6 # roll the die five times res <- sample(die,5, replace = TRUE) #count the sixes count <- sum((res == 6)) } sim_rolls <- replicate(10^5, roll_die_five()) We can now plot a histogram of the outcomes: qplot(sim_rolls, binwidth = 1) We could do the same experiment now with only one line of code by using the rbinom() function. sim_roll_with_rbinom <- rbinom(10^5,5,1/6) Now let us compare the histogram: qplot(sim_roll_with_rbinom, binwidth = 1) The probability to get 3 sixes according to the simulation (relative frequency) is: sum(sim_rolls == 3)/length(sim_rolls) ## [1] 0.03236 This could be also be compute directly by dbinom()like this: dbinom(3, 5, 1/6) ## [1] 0.03215021 I think you start getting the idea. We now know more functionality which can substitute for basic approaches we built ourselves from scratch already before. However, once you go to developing code for industrial strength simulations and more realistic real world applications, speed and performance will become essential. Using built in functions whenever possible will support this goal. We will talk a bit more systematically about efficient and fast computation in the next lecture. 4.6.3 Modeling asset price dynamics with binomial trees The investment problem we analyzed in this lecture has only one period. Most real world investment problems fluctuate and can be traded frequently. They pay dividends, face fluctuating interest rates and are subject to various other uncertainties over time. The binomial distribution while conceptually very simple, allows to model asset price fluctuations conveniently and realistically. In Finance there are two basic workhorse models to represent asset price dynamics: Binomial lattices and Ito processes. Both are rooted in probability but binomial lattices are conceptually simpler. Yet they form an excellent basis for computational work associated with investment problems. To define a binomial lattice model, a basic period of length has to be established, such as a day or a week. According to the model, if the price is known at the beginning of a period, the price at the beginning of the next period is one of only two possible values. Usually these two possible values are defined as multiples of the price in the previous period, a multiple \(u\) for up and a multiple \(d\) for down. Both \(u\) and \(d\) are positive with \(u > 1\) and usually \(d < 1\). Hence, if the price at the beginning of the period is \(S\), it will be either \(uS\) or \(dS\) at the next period. The probabilities of these outcomes are \(p\) and \(1-p\) respectively, for some \(p\) with \(0 < p < 1\). This model continues for many time steps. If the time step are short, there are many possibilities for the price at the end of the process, even though the possibilities at each time step are confined to only two. Figure 4.5: A binomial lattice with three time steps To specify such a binomial lattice model, we must select values for \(u\) and \(d\) and the probability \(p\). These should be chosen such as to reflect the true stochastic nature of the stock price as faithfully as possible. Since the model is multiplicative and \(u > 0\) and \(d > 0\), the price will never become negative. Accordingly, we can define \(\nu\) as the expected annual growth rate by \[\begin{equation*} \nu = E\left( \ln(S_T/S_0) \right) \end{equation*}\] where \(S_0\) is the initial stock price and \(S_T\) is the stock price after a year. Likewise we define \(\sigma\) as the annual standard deviation of the growth rate \[\begin{equation*} \sigma^2 = Var\left( \ln(S_T/S_0) \right) \end{equation*}\] If we choose a period of \(\Delta t\), which is small compared to 1, the parameters of the binomial lattice can be selected as \[\begin{eqnarray*} p&=&\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\nu}{\sigma} \right) \sqrt{\Delta t} \\ u&=&\exp(\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}) \\ d&=&\exp(-\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}) \end{eqnarray*}\] We are not able to go into the details of why this works, we can claim here only that with this choice, the binomial model will closely match the value of \(\nu\) and \(\sigma\). The closeness of the match improves if \(\Delta t\) is made smaller and smaller becoming exact as \(\Delta t\) goes to zero. Now let us check this using our R-tools learned so far. Take again the S&P 500 as our test data set. Remember our SP500 data we used before: head(sp500, n=5) ## # A tibble: 5 x 9 ## symbol date open high low close volume adjusted returns ## <chr> <date> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ## 1 ^GSPC 2011-01-03 1258. 1276. 1258. 1272. 4286670000 1272. NA ## 2 ^GSPC 2011-01-04 1273. 1274. 1263. 1270. 4796420000 1270. -0.00131 ## 3 ^GSPC 2011-01-05 1269. 1278. 1265. 1277. 4764920000 1277. 0.00501 ## 4 ^GSPC 2011-01-06 1276. 1278. 1270. 1274. 4844100000 1274. -0.00212 ## 5 ^GSPC 2011-01-07 1274. 1277. 1262. 1272. 4963110000 1272. -0.00184 We first need the annual returns. In order to do so we automatically extract the first price in each year. This can be done in many ways. Here is a solution, that works: We first extract the year from the data column and add a new variable to the sp500 data frame. For this we use a function from the lubridate package. This function is called year() and if applied to a date object extracts the year only. Then we split the data frame according to the year using R’s slit() function. This will create a list of data frames of daily records, where each list element contains the records of one year. We then write a little function, which takes a data frame as argument and selects the first row. Finally we apply this function to each element of the list using R’s function lapply(). Finally we bind all observations together with the do.call() function. This is admittedly a piece of advanced R acrobatics, so if this sounds weird or difficult to comprehend, don’t worry. # add a column with the year using year function from lubridate package sp500$year <- lubridate::year(sp500$date) # split dataframe according to year split_by_year <- split(sp500, sp500$year) # write a function selecting the first row of a given data frame sel <- function(x){x[1,]} # apply this function to each element of the list. aux <- lapply(split_by_year, sel) # recombine the list entries into a data frame but drop the returns column sp500_annual <- do.call(rbind, aux) |> subset(select = -returns) head(sp500_annual, n = 3) ## # A tibble: 3 x 9 ## symbol date open high low close volume adjusted year ## <chr> <date> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ## 1 ^GSPC 2011-01-03 1258. 1276. 1258. 1272. 4286670000 1272. 2011 ## 2 ^GSPC 2012-01-03 1259. 1285. 1259. 1277. 3943710000 1277. 2012 ## 3 ^GSPC 2013-01-02 1426. 1462. 1426. 1462. 4202600000 1462. 2013 Now we can compute annual log returns: sp500_annual$returns <- c(NA, diff(log(sp500_annual$adjusted), lag = 1)) Now we are ready to compute \(\nu\) and \(\sigma^2\). nu <- mean(sp500_annual$returns, na.rm = T) sigma <- sqrt(var(sp500_annual$returns, na.rm = T)) nu ## [1] 0.106802 sigma ## [1] 0.1070446 Now we wish to make a binomial price model of the sp500 based on these data for a daily period. Assume the year has 250 trading days. Then we get: u <- exp(sigma*sqrt(1/250)) d <- exp(- sigma*sqrt(1/250)) p <- 1/2*(1 +(nu/sigma)*sqrt(1/250)) u ## [1] 1.006793 d ## [1] 0.9932528 p ## [1] 0.5315511 We can now generate a binomial tree by a function: binomial_tree <- function(S0, up, down, steps){ # initialize tree as a matrix tree <- matrix(0, nrow = steps+1, ncol = steps + 1) # fill up tree in a double for-loop for(i in 1:(steps + 1)){ for(j in (1:i)){ tree[i,j] <- S0*up^(j -1)*down^((i-1)-(j-1)) } } return(tree) } Lets generate a tree with three steps: binomial_tree(S0=100, up = u, down = d, steps = 3) ## [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] ## [1,] 100.00000 0.00000 0.0000 0.0000 ## [2,] 99.32528 100.67931 0.0000 0.0000 ## [3,] 98.65511 100.00000 101.3632 0.0000 ## [4,] 97.98946 99.32528 100.6793 102.0518 How is this related to the binomial distribution? The random variable, which we are studying is a random variable \(X\) with \(P(X = u) = p\) and $P(X = d) = 1-p $. The stock price \(S\) follows the process \[\begin{equation*} S_{n+1} = S_n X_{n+1} \quad \text{for}\,\, n \geq 0 \end{equation*}\] Thus given the value of \(S_n\) \[\begin{equation*} S_{n+1} = \begin{cases} u\,S_n \, \mbox{with probability} \,\,p \\ d\, S_n \, \mbox{with probability}\,\, (1-p) \end{cases} \mbox{for}\,\, n \geq 0 \end{equation*}\] independent of the past. Thus in each time period the stock price goes either up or down and the randomness comes from identical independently distributed bernoulli random variables. If we expand the recursion for the stock price we get \[\begin{equation*} S_n = S_0 \times X_1 \times \cdots \times X_n, \,\,\, n\geq 0 \end{equation*}\] From this expansion we can conclude that for a given \(n\) \[\begin{equation*} S_n = u^i d^{(n-i)} S_0 \,\,\, \mbox{for some} \,\,\, i \in \{1, \cdots, n\} \end{equation*}\] meaning that the stock price went up \(i\) times and down \((n-i)\) times with the corresponding probabilities determined by \[\begin{equation*} P(S_n = u^i d^{(n-i)} S_0) = \binom{n}{i}p^i\,(1-p)^{(n-i)},\,\,\, 0\leq i \leq n \end{equation*}\] Let us use these insights and what we learned about handling of binomial random variables in R by simulating an asset price process for the sp500 for a year - i.e. for 250 daily time steps and plot the probability mass function of the resulting price distribution. # Simulate 250 time steps of the asset price process starting with S_0 = 100. tree <- binomial_tree(S0=100, up = u, down = d, steps = 250) # Select # the last row of the resulting matrix representing the final outcomes after 250 time steps. outcomes <- (tree[dim(tree)[1], ]) success <- 0:250 # plot the probability distribution of outcomes plot(outcomes, dbinom(success, size = 250, prob = p), type = "h") abline(v=sum(outcomes*dbinom(success, size = 250, prob = p)),col="blue",lwd=3) abline(v = 110.6802, col = "red", lwd = 2) The blue vertical line shows the expected value of the asset value after a year, given the simulated asset price process. The red line shows the expected value, of an asset multiplied by the expected annual return, we have extracted from the data and which we have used to calibrate our binomial pricing model. You will encounter this model of asset price dynamics often in your career in Finance, in particular, when it comes to pricing derivatives. 4.7 Project: Credit risk model and independence In this project you will study the probability of credit risk, the risk that one or more creditors in a portfolio of loans will default. We have already touched a bit of this problem in our discussion of the role of the assumptions about dependence and independence of risks in structured finance. Clearly in banking, credit risk and its analysis is the key issue, since banking is basically about borrowing and lending of money. In this project you will discover that the probability concepts we have covered in this lecture and in the previous lectures allow you to already go very far towards a deeper understanding of credit risk. Let us now make use of your knowledge of R and probability to make a first step towards modelling credit default and portfolio losses. We will take a common and time proven strategy in problem solving, when we face a very complex and difficult situation like this. It is usually a good idea to think about a similar but simpler situation first, and then build from there. Let me help you with the simplification. Let us make the following assumptions. We fix a time horizon \(T\), in credit risk this would be something like one year - for example - and in addition we assume: that our portfolio consists of \(N\) identical borrowers. In credit risk lingo these borrowers are also often referred to as obligors. All obligors have an exposure of size \(L\) (some Euro amount for instance) and when one obligor defaults, your bank for which you risk manage the loan portfolio, will be able to recover a fraction \(c\), called the recovery rate. All defaults over \(T\) happen independently with the same probability of default \(p\). Already now we know that this is a simplification which puts us far away from reality. Still it is useful for us to build a step by step understanding of the problem. The key point is not to forget about this simplification and how it can be dropped later when we can build already on some of our experience with the simpler problem. This is by the way quite a common and good strategy both to solve analytical as well as programming problems. Here we develop a bit of practice with this habit. Use R and your knowledge about the binomial distribution to formulate a model for a random variable \(X\) describing the number of defaults that occur until \(T\) and produce a plot of the probability distribution of losses from default assuming a portfolio size \(N\) of 100 and individual default probabilities \(p = 5 \%\). Now let us think a bit what is needed, if we want to get away from our oversimplified framework we have so far and take a step towards more realism. For such reflections it is often a good idea to drop superfluous structure for the question at hand. This is also a time proven strategy in problem solving. Ask yourself, what is the minimal setup I need to make my point. This is a question of the right amount of simplifcation, where the goal is to be as simple as possible but not to oversimplify. Let me help you a bit with this tactics: Assume now you have only two instead of \(N\) obligors in your portfolio. Let us call them borrower \(A\) and borrower \(B\). You would like to think about the risk they could default on their obligations over a time horizon \(T\), say 1 year from now. Your staff has produced intelligence from a detailed data analysis and has provided you with the the numbers \(p_A\), the probability that the first borrower will default within the time horizon \(T\) as well as \(p_B\), the probability that the second borrower will default over the horizon \(T\). Would it help you towards more realism, if you had this information? Would it help you to find out The probability that \(A\) and \(B\) will default before \(T\), denoted \(P_{AB}\) (joint probability of default). Note that this would entail the knowledge of the probability that \(A\) defaults before \(T\) given that \(B\) has defaulted before \(T\), or the probability that \(B\) has defaults given that \(A\) has defaulted. Let us denote these conditional probabilities by \(p_{A|B}\) and \(p_{B|A}\). The correlation coefficient \(\rho_{AB}\) between the event that \(A\) defaults and \(B\) defaults. Going back to your credit risk model, what does it tell you about extreme cases? What is the probability that all \(N\) obligors default? What is the probability that none defaults? Note that these extreme situations are still compatible with the assumption that each obligor defaults with probability \(5 \%\). Can you recover any information about the likelihood of joint defaults in these two extreme situations?
